one_line_takeaway: LLM-based intent-verification judges intercept 93-98% of synthetic prompt-injection/RAG-poisoning tool calls at ~1 inference call's latency cost, but plugin permission violations (75-94% IR), realistic low-prevalence PPV (22.7% at best), and hand-crafted adversarial evasion (dropping IR to 50-63%) show intent verification alone is not a sufficient gatekeeper -- consistent with the paper's own architectural argument for layered sandboxing, zero-trust tokens, and audit logging rather than a single defense.
key_finding: All five LLM judges intercept >=93% of TC1/TC2 malicious calls (93.0-98.5% IR) versus <10% for the NLI baselines (BART-MNLI AUC=0.75 ZH/0.88 EN but only 8-10% binary IR; mDeBERTa-NLI ~0% IR, AUC~0.50-0.56), demonstrating instruction-following LLM judges are categorically better than off-the-shelf entailment models at tool-call authorization. Qwen2.5-14B gives the best local balance (98.2-98.5% IR, 9.7-20.1% FPR); the cloud cascade Qwen3.5-9B->GPT-4o-mini achieves 91.9-92.6% IR at 1.9-6.7% FPR, and the fully local cascade Qwen3.5-9B->Qwen2.5-14B achieves 94.7-95.6% IR at 6.0-9.7% FPR for data-sovereign deployments (differences confirmed significant by McNemar's test, Table 8). TC3 (malicious plugins that perform a legitimate action alongside a covert side-channel) is markedly harder: IR among judges with a real precision-recall balance is 75-94% (GPT-4o-mini: 75.0% ZH / 94.2% EN; Qwen2.5-14B: 77.5% ZH / 88.3% EN; Qwen3.5-9B: 77.5% both languages), with a striking, only partially explained 19pp cross-lingual asymmetry for GPT-4o-mini that a JSON-key-translation ablation failed to explain (translating keys made GPT-4o-mini's ZH IR worse, -7.5pp, ruling out key-language mismatch as the cause). The end-to-end 4-layer pipeline (n=100) achieves 96.0% IR / 16.0% FPR at P50=980ms total latency, of which L1+L3+L4 together contribute only ~18ms (P50) -- the LLM judge call dominates cost. Generalization to InjecAgent (100 sampled, indirect-injection cases) yields 100% IR for GPT-4o-mini and 99% for Qwen3.5-9B. Two results substantially qualify the headline numbers: (1) under realistic 1% attack prevalence, even the best single judge (GPT-4o-mini ZH, FPR=3.2%) has only 22.7% PPV -- roughly 3 of 4 blocks are false alarms (Table 7, Eq. 2) -- motivating the paper's argument that L2 must not be the sole gatekeeper; (2) a small (n=30) hand-crafted adversarial-evasion probe (double negation, task disguise, authority injection) drops IR to 50-63% versus 93-98% on the standard benchmark, with authority injection the hardest subtype (30-50% IR).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (single-author, Yuxu Ge, University of York) is the Layered Governance Architecture (LGA): a four-layer defense-in-depth stack for autonomous LLM agent systems consisting of L1 execution sandboxing (OS-level isolation via unshare namespaces in the prototype, Docker/seccomp envisioned for production), L2 intent verification (an independent judge model checks each proposed tool call against the original task before execution), L3 zero-trust inter-agent authorization (minimum-privilege capability tokens with TTL/scope limits, HMAC-SHA256-authenticated and JSON-Schema-validated inter-agent messages), and L4 immutable append-only audit logging. The paper formalizes three execution-layer threat classes with explicit mathematical definitions (agency abuse via prompt injection, RAG data poisoning, malicious skill plugins), builds an original bilingual (Chinese + machine-translated English) benchmark of 1,081 tool-call samples to evaluate L2 specifically, runs a case study auditing the open-source agent framework OpenClaw against the four-layer compliance rubric (finding four governance gaps: soft SOUL.md constraints, self-declared plugin permissions with no runtime verification, lack of intra-directory permission partitioning, and a mutable audit log), and runs a 100-sample end-to-end pipeline evaluation of all four layers together plus a generalization check on the external InjecAgent benchmark.
threat_model: Black-box, non-adaptive adversary who can (TC1) inject content into agent inputs (direct or indirect/document-poisoned prompt injection), (TC2) poison shared knowledge-base entries to corrupt retrieval-augmented decisions, or (TC3) publish malicious skill plugins whose runtime behavior exceeds self-declared permissions -- but who has no knowledge of the deployed judge model or its parameters. The paper explicitly scopes out adaptive attackers who iteratively optimize inputs against a known judge, flagging this as future work. Formal definitions are given for each threat class (Defs. 4.1-4.3) in terms of an agent behavior function mapping inputs/state to tool-invocation sequences and an authorized-tool-call set.
limitations: The paper itself lists seven limitations (Sect. 7.4.5): (i) the benchmark is synthetically generated by GPT-4o-mini and may not reflect real adversarial creativity; (ii) GPT-4o-mini is both the benchmark generator and one of the evaluated judges, a circularity the author bounds but does not eliminate by comparing against an independently-developed model (Qwen3.5-9B), noting GPT-4o-mini actually underperforms Qwen3.5-9B on Chinese IR (93.0% vs 96.5%), though a deeper structural bias on FPR 'cannot be excluded' without regenerating the benchmark with an independent generator (e.g., Claude or Gemini); (iii) the end-to-end pipeline evaluation covers only 100 English samples with a single (cloud) judge -- no local-judge end-to-end run exists; (iv) the English dataset is machine-translated from Chinese, and a native-English TC3 ablation (n=40) shows IR 11-17pp lower than the translated version, suggesting translation artifacts may inflate reported EN detection rates; (v) Qwen3.5-9B's reported latency reflects an unoptimized Ollama configuration (num_predict=50); capping output to 10 tokens gives a 6.5x speedup with negligible IR change; (vi) the cross-lingual TC3 asymmetry is not fully explained -- the key-translation ablation ruled out the leading hypothesis; (vii) the paper's own adversarial-evasion probe (n=30, hand-crafted) shows the approach is not robust to even simple evasion patterns (IR drops to 50-63%), and the threat model explicitly excludes adaptive attackers who know the judge. Additional caveats surfaced in the text itself: all cascade security metrics are post-hoc compositions of independently-collected per-sample predictions rather than live two-stage inference; sample sizes for several subgroup breakdowns are small (n=30-40 per TC3 subtype/language cell, n=100 for the end-to-end pipeline, n=100 sampled from InjecAgent), and OpenClaw label verification/compliance assessment rests on a single inspector's manual review and a single author's static code analysis of one commit.

datasets_used: Author-constructed bilingual (Chinese original + gpt-4o-mini machine-translated English) benchmark of 1,081 tool-call samples: 504 benign (464 non-plugin + 40 benign plugin), 457 TC1/TC2 malicious (141 direct injection, 180 indirect injection, 136 RAG poisoning), 120 TC3 malicious plugin calls (30 each: exfiltration, privilege escalation, persistence, supply-chain)

External InjecAgent benchmark (Zhan et al. 2024): 1,054 indirect-prompt-injection test cases across 17 user tools and 62 attacker tools; 100 sampled (fixed seed=42) for the paper's generalization check
OpenClaw open-source agent framework source code (commit f014e25) used for the static-analysis compliance case study
quantitative_results: All five LLM judges: 93.0-98.5% IR on TC1/TC2 (n=457); NLI baselines <10% IR (BART-MNLI 8.1-9.6%, mDeBERTa-NLI ~0%)
Qwen2.5-14B: 98.2% IR / 9.7% FPR (ZH), 98.5% IR / 20.1% FPR (EN) -- best local single-model balance
Cascade Qwen3.5-9B->GPT-4o-mini: 91.9% IR / 1.9% FPR (ZH), 92.6% IR / 6.7% FPR (EN)
Cascade Qwen3.5-9B->Qwen2.5-14B (fully local): 95.6% IR / 6.0% FPR (ZH), 94.7% IR / 9.7% FPR (EN)
TC3 (plugin) IR: GPT-4o-mini 75.0% ZH / 94.2% EN; Qwen2.5-14B 77.5% ZH / 88.3% EN; Qwen3.5-9B 77.5% both languages
PPV at 1% attack prevalence: GPT-4o-mini ZH 22.7% (best single judge); Cascade Q->G ZH 32.8%; Qwen3.5-9B ZH only 2.8%
End-to-end 4-layer pipeline (n=100): 96.0% IR, 16.0% FPR, F_su=89.6%, P50 total latency=980ms (L2 judge ~966ms; L1+L3+L4 combined ~18ms)
L1/L3/L4 isolated microbenchmarks: L1 sandbox spawn P50=0.95ms; L3 HMAC+schema check P50<0.02ms; L4 audit fsync write P50=4.1ms
InjecAgent generalization (n=100 sampled): GPT-4o-mini 100.0% IR [95% CI 96.4-100%]; Qwen3.5-9B 99.0% IR [95% CI 94.6-100%]
Adversarial-evasion probe (n=30 hand-crafted): IR drops to 50% (GPT-4o-mini) and 63% (Qwen3.5-9B and Qwen2.5-14B), versus 93-98% on the standard benchmark
num_predict output-cap ablation: Qwen3.5-9B P50 latency 1,981ms->303ms (6.5x) with IR 96.5%->96.3% and FPR 34.1%->29.3%
baselines_compared: Unverified baseline (no verification, all calls allowed) -- 0% IR by construction
mDeBERTa-v3-base-xnli (zero-shot NLI, ~280MB, CPU, evaluated binary on Chinese only)
